text: of the spleen for non-parasitic splenic cyst by Dr. Parnasa from Rome. Good afternoon. I would like to thank SAGES for the opportunity to present this video, which is about robot-assisted minimally invasive partial resection of the spleen.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the gastrocolic ligament and control small gastric vessels. The dissection of the main trunk of the splenic artery is performed with robotic assistance.

The surgeon clamps the main trunk of the splenic artery to reduce the spleen's volume, facilitating safer dissection. The first and second small arterial branches are reconnected to preserve vascularization of the lower spleen, which remains intact. The main trunk of the artery is controlled with a clip, and the clamp is removed to verify the correct dissection of the arterial branches.

The main trunk of the splenic vein is visible, detached from the tail of the pancreas and encircled by a vessel loop for traction. The surgeon gently detaches the tail of the pancreas from the vessels, grasping the main trunk of the artery and vein. The ascending artery and vein are divided, revealing the lower pole of the spleen recovering its vascularization. The vascular hilum of the upper pole of the spleen is detached from the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform parenchymal transection along the demarcation line, minimizing bleeding. The lower pole of the spleen is separated from the upper pole, which contains a large cystic lesion. Bipolar coagulation controls small bleeding from the stump, and both inferior and superior ligaments of the spleen are divided. The vessel loops are removed, and the upper part of the spleen is extracted through a small incision using an endobag.

The surgeon reconstructs the splenophrenic ligament to prevent torsion of the remnant spleen on its vascular pole. A hemostatic matrix is applied to control small bleeding from the remaining spleen tissue, completing the procedure.

Are you vaccinating the patients even though your intent is to leave splenic tissue behind? No. But are you providing vaccination to the patient even though you're intending to leave immune function? No.

because I'm confident on the remnant spleen to keep the immunological protection. And what percentage of spleen do you think that you have to leave behind, or what weight of remnant do you think you have to have to preserve adequate immune function? In literature, I found that about twenty percent of the parenchymal spleen is enough. we have to consider that the cystic lesion is very huge and occupy the entire upper pole of the spleen. So it's about fifty percent, I think, the parenchymal residual. Do you observe growth of the spleen after this? You said you follow the map six months later with ultrasound. Does the spleen volume increases with time? This operation was performed about eight months ago. So we performed the first control of the remnant spleen after six months. So the spleen was constant, was about the same size I left after the operation. I plan other controls in the future to verify the growth of the spleen.
